---
title: Call another context
description: Dispatch a cross-context call, gate the entry point, and verify the caller's origin.
sidebar:
  order: 4
---

import { Aside, Steps, CardGrid, LinkCard } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';
import SeqDiagram from '../../../../components/SeqDiagram.astro';

**Task:** have a method in one context invoke a method in another context.

A cross-context call (`xcall`) is one-way and asynchronous: you queue a call to a
target context's method, and the node delivers it. The model — and why origin is
trustworthy — is in the [cross-context reference](/protocol/xcall/). This guide
is the three moving parts from `apps/xcall-example`.

## 1. Dispatch the call

`env::xcall(context_id, function, params)` queues a call. Encode arguments as the
JSON the target method expects:

```rust
use calimero_sdk::{app, ContextId};

fn xcall_to(&mut self, target_context: ContextId, method: &str) -> app::Result<()> {
    let current_context = ContextId::from(calimero_sdk::env::context_id());

    #[derive(calimero_sdk::serde::Serialize)]
    #[serde(crate = "calimero_sdk::serde")]
    struct Params {
        from_context: ContextId,
    }

    let params = calimero_sdk::serde_json::to_vec(&Params {
        from_context: current_context,
    })?;

    calimero_sdk::env::xcall(target_context.as_ref(), method, &params);
    Ok(())
}
```

## 2. Gate the entry point

A method is only reachable by `xcall` if you mark it `#[app::xcall]`. The node
refuses to dispatch a cross-context call to any method without this attribute —
so undecorated methods stay private to direct callers.

## 3. Verify the origin

`env::xcall_origin()` returns the calling context id — set by the node, so it
**cannot be forged**. It is `None` for a direct (non-xcall) call. Use it to
reject direct calls and to confirm the caller is who the payload claims:

```rust
#[app::xcall]
pub fn pong(&mut self, from_context: ContextId) -> app::Result<()> {
    let origin = calimero_sdk::env::xcall_origin().map(ContextId::from);

    let Some(origin) = origin else {
        app::bail!("pong is xcall-only: no cross-context origin (direct call rejected)");
    };
    if origin != from_context {
        app::bail!(
            "xcall provenance mismatch: node-set origin {} != claimed from_context {}",
            origin,
            from_context
        );
    }

    self.counter.increment()?;
    Ok(())
}
```

<Aside type="caution" title="Don't trust the payload alone">
  `from_context` here is self-reported by the caller. The trustworthy value is
  `env::xcall_origin()`; comparing the two is what makes the call authentic.
</Aside>

Putting the three parts together — dispatch queues the call, the node delivers
it, and the gated entry point verifies the node-set origin before doing any
work. The call is one-way: nothing returns to the caller.

<SeqDiagram id="d-build-xcall-dispatch" title="Dispatch a cross-context call"
  lanes={[
    { label: 'Caller ctx', color: '#3fb950' },
    { label: 'Runtime', color: '#a371f7' },
    { label: 'Target ctx', color: '#58a6ff' },
  ]}
  steps={[
    { from: 0, to: 1, label: 'env::xcall(target, "pong", params)' },
    { from: 1, to: 1, label: 'queue call (one-way, async)' },
    { from: 1, to: 2, label: 'dispatch pong(from_context)' },
    { from: 2, to: 2, label: 'check #[app::xcall] gate' },
    { from: 2, to: 1, label: 'xcall_origin()', dashed: true },
    { from: 1, to: 2, label: 'node-set caller ctx id', dashed: true },
    { from: 2, to: 2, label: 'verify origin == from_context' },
    { from: 2, to: 2, label: 'counter.increment()' },
    { type: 'divider', label: 'one-way: no return to caller' },
  ]} />

## Exercise it

```sh
# from the source context, ping the target's `pong` entry point
meroctl --node node1 call ping \
  --context <source-context-id> --args '{"target_context": "<target-context-id>"}'
```

The two contexts must run the same application and share a namespace.
